content.text: What Task-Driven Agents Are

A task-driven agent operates on a queue of discrete, well-defined work items rather than an open-ended goal or a live conversation. Each task is an independent unit with a description, an expected output, optional dependencies on prior tasks, a retry policy, and a timeout. The agent picks tasks from the queue, executes them with tools, stores results, and marks tasks complete. This pattern is a direct architectural match for automation workflows, batch processing, and multi-step pipelines where the work items can be enumerated upfront — in contrast to goal-driven agents that discover sub-tasks at runtime.
Task Schema and Status Machine

A task transitions through a fixed set of statuses: PENDING → RUNNING → DONE or FAILED, with optional CANCELLED. Storing status explicitly — rather than inferring it from code flow — enables persistence, resumability, and observability. A failed task can be retried (transition back to PENDING) up to a configured limit before being marked permanently failed.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any, Optional, Callable
import uuid, time

class TaskStatus(Enum):
    PENDING   = "pending"
    RUNNING   = "running"
    DONE      = "done"
    FAILED    = "failed"
    CANCELLED = "cancelled"

@dataclass
class TaskSpec:
    """Immutable task definition."""
    description: str
    input_data: dict[str, Any]
    executor: str          # name of the executor function to use
    depends_on: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # task ids
    max_retries: int = 2
    timeout_seconds: int = 60
    priority: int = 5      # 1 (highest) … 10 (lowest)

@dataclass
class TaskRecord:
    """Mutable runtime state for one task."""
    id: str
    spec: TaskSpec
    status: TaskStatus = TaskStatus.PENDING
    result: Optional[Any] = None
    error: Optional[str] = None
    attempts: int = 0
    created_at: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    started_at: Optional[float] = None
    finished_at: Optional[float] = None
    
    @staticmethod
    def create(spec: TaskSpec) -> "TaskRecord":
        return TaskRecord(id=str(uuid.uuid4())[:8], spec=spec)
    
    def can_retry(self) -> bool:
        return self.attempts < self.spec.max_retries
    
    def duration(self) -> Optional[float]:
        if self.started_at and self.finished_at:
            return self.finished_at - self.started_at
        return None
Task Registry and Dependency Resolution

Tasks with dependencies form a DAG (directed acyclic graph). The scheduler must only release a task for execution when all its dependency tasks are in DONE status. A topological sort over the DAG gives the valid execution order; any task whose dependencies are all done is eligible to run, enabling parallelism where the graph structure permits.
Executors: Mapping Task Types to LLM Chains

Each task type has a named executor — a function that takes the task's input data and returns a result string. Executors are registered in a map keyed by the executor name stored in the task spec. This allows different task types (summarise, classify, extract, generate) to use different prompts and tools while sharing the same scheduler and registry infrastructure.
The Task Runner

The task runner iterates over ready tasks, dispatches them to the correct executor, handles errors and retries, and marks results. For independent tasks, it can run them concurrently using a thread pool or asyncio — the DAG structure tells it which tasks are safe to parallelize.
End-to-End Example

A document processing pipeline: extract title and author from each document, classify the document type, then summarise it. The summarise step depends on the classify step completing first — a two-level dependency chain.

registry = TaskRegistry()

document = """
AutoGPT is an autonomous AI agent developed by Toran Bruce Richards in March 2023.
It uses GPT-4 to chain language model calls and execute tasks autonomously without
human intervention, using tools like web search, file I/O, and code execution.
"""

# Step 1: extract metadata (no dependencies)
extract_title = registry.add(TaskSpec(
    description="Extract document title/subject",
    input_data={"text": document, "field": "main subject or title"},
    executor="extract",
    priority=1,
))

extract_author = registry.add(TaskSpec(
    description="Extract author name",
    input_data={"text": document, "field": "author name"},
    executor="extract",
    priority=1,
))

# Step 2: classify (no dependencies, can run in parallel with step 1)
classify_task = registry.add(TaskSpec(
    description="Classify document type",
    input_data={
        "text": document,
        "categories": ["technical", "news", "tutorial", "research"],
    },
    executor="classify",
    priority=2,
))

# Step 3: summarise (depends on classify completing)
summarise_task = registry.add(TaskSpec(
    description="Summarise document",
    input_data={"text": document},
    executor="summarise",
    depends_on=[classify_task.id],  # wait for classification
    priority=3,
))

# Run all tasks (extract + classify run in parallel; summarise waits)
summary = run_all_tasks(registry, max_workers=3)
print(summary)
# → {'done': 4}

# Retrieve results
for task in registry._tasks.values():
    print(f"{task.spec.description}: {task.result}")
# Extract main subject: AutoGPT
# Extract author name: Toran Bruce Richards
# Classify document type: news
# Summarise document: AutoGPT is an autonomous AI agent...
Testing Task-Driven Agents

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch

def test_topological_order():
    reg = TaskRegistry()
    a = reg.add(TaskSpec("A", {}, "summarise"))
    b = reg.add(TaskSpec("B", {}, "summarise", depends_on=[a.id]))
    c = reg.add(TaskSpec("C", {}, "summarise", depends_on=[b.id]))
    order = reg.topological_order()
    assert order.index(a.id) < order.index(b.id) < order.index(c.id)

def test_cycle_detection():
    reg = TaskRegistry()
    a = reg.add(TaskSpec("A", {}, "summarise"))
    b = reg.add(TaskSpec("B", {}, "summarise", depends_on=[a.id]))
    # Manually inject a cycle
    reg._tasks[a.id].spec.depends_on.append(b.id)
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Cycle detected"):
        reg.topological_order()

def test_ready_tasks_respects_deps():
    reg = TaskRegistry()
    a = reg.add(TaskSpec("A", {}, "summarise"))
    b = reg.add(TaskSpec("B", {}, "summarise", depends_on=[a.id]))
    ready = reg.ready_tasks()
    assert len(ready) == 1
    assert ready[0].id == a.id
    # Mark A done
    reg.update_status(a.id, TaskStatus.DONE, result="ok")
    ready2 = reg.ready_tasks()
    assert len(ready2) == 1
    assert ready2[0].id == b.id

def test_retry_on_failure():
    reg = TaskRegistry()
    t = reg.add(TaskSpec("T", {"text": "hello"}, "summarise", max_retries=2))
    
    call_count = 0
    def flaky_executor(input_data):
        nonlocal call_count
        call_count += 1
        if call_count < 3:
            raise RuntimeError("transient error")
        return "success"
    
    with patch.dict(EXECUTOR_REGISTRY, {"summarise": flaky_executor}):
        run_all_tasks(reg, max_workers=1)
    
    assert reg._tasks[t.id].status == TaskStatus.DONE
    assert reg._tasks[t.id].result == "success"
    assert call_count == 3
Summary
Task-driven agents process a queue of discrete, typed work items — each with an executor name, input data, dependency list, and retry policy.
Dependencies form a DAG; topological sort determines valid execution order, and parallel tasks run concurrently when their dependencies are all DONE.
Executors are named functions registered in a map, allowing different task types to use different LLM chains without changing the scheduler logic.
Failed tasks are retried up to max_retries; tasks whose dependency has permanently failed are automatically cancelled to prevent deadlocks.
